one_line_takeaway: By profiling safety/harmful experts from response activations (not prompts) and directly optimizing a ternary loss over pre-Top-K router probabilities, RouteHijack turns MoE routing sparsity itself into a jailbreak attack surface, reaching 69.3% average ASR input-only across seven MoE LLMs (3x GCG, 2x the best white-box expert-intervention baseline) with only 1.3% utility loss, and the resulting suffixes transfer zero-shot across sibling models and into MoE-based VLMs.
key_finding: RouteHijack achieves 69.3% average ASR (peak 89.1% on DeepSeek-MoE-16B-Chat) across the 7 target models, versus 7.1% for the no-attack baseline, 21.9% for GCG, 33.3% for SAFEx, and 17.0% for SteerMoE -- i.e., more than double the best white-box baseline (SAFEx) and more than triple GCG, despite RouteHijack being input-only while SAFEx/SteerMoE require inference-time model intervention. Utility cost is small: average absolute score drop of 2.4 pts (CoLA), 1.9 (RTE), 0.3 (WinoGrande), 1.7 (OpenBookQA), 0.2 (ARC-Challenge), averaging 1.3% overall (vs. 10.8% average drop, with CoLA collapsing ~30 pts, when the utility-aware filtering ablation is removed). Mechanistic checks show the attack really moves routing, not just prompt distraction: average boundary-token TESR of -28.75% and THPR of +28.51%, with global (across-generation) safety-expert frequency down 23.44% and harmful-expert frequency up 22.87% on average. Ablations show each ternary-loss term is necessary: removing Lpromote drops ASR to 50.2%, removing Lsuppress drops it to 7.1% with 6/7 models suffering semantic collapse (>50% disordered outputs), removing Lrefusal drops it to 29.1%. Response-driven profiling (69.3% avg ASR) substantially outperforms prompt-driven profiling (30.5% avg ASR). Transfer results: suffix optimized on a source model raises average ASR from 27.7% (baseline on sibling models) to 61.2% across reasoning, DPO/preference, code, and domain-adapted sibling variants with zero re-optimization; and from 2.47% to 38.7% average ASR when transferred zero-shot to three MoE-based VLMs by rendering the text suffix into an image, despite the suffix having been optimized purely on text.
core_contribution: RouteHijack is a routing-aware jailbreak attack against Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLMs that operates under a realistic input-only (no weight/logit access at deployment) threat model. Its own contribution has two parts: (1) response-driven contrastive profiling, which localizes safety-critical and harmful experts by contrasting router activation frequencies on teacher-forced safe-refusal vs. harmful-completion continuations of the same malicious query (rather than contrasting on prompts, which the paper shows is confounded by topic/style), plus a utility-aware filtering step that penalizes safety-expert candidates that also fire on a benign corpus to avoid targeting polysemantic experts; and (2) a ternary-loss adversarial-suffix optimization (Lsuppress + Lpromote + Lrefusal, weighted 3:1:1) that backpropagates through the pre-Top-K softmax router distribution at the final prompt token to jointly suppress identified safety experts, push cumulative routing mass on harmful experts past a bounded margin (m_harm=0.3, to avoid expert-collapse/gibberish), and penalize refusal-token likelihood over the first W=5 decoding steps. The suffix is optimized offline (white-box) on a surrogate open-weight model via a GCG-style gradient-guided discrete search (candidate batch 128, top-k=256, suffix length T=20, 300 steps) and deployed as a pure input-level suffix with no access to the target's weights, activations, or inference code.
threat_model: Two-stage, proxy-based transfer attack, explicitly scoped by the authors in Section 3. Offline stage: adversary has full white-box access (weights, activations, router logits) to a related open-weight MoE model (e.g., same family/checkpoint lineage as the target) and uses it to localize experts and optimize a universal adversarial suffix. Deployment stage: attack is strictly input-level -- the adversary can only submit text prompts containing the discrete suffix and cannot modify weights, prune experts, or alter inference code of the target system (contrasted explicitly with invasive baselines SteerMoE/SAFEx/L3, which require inference-time model intervention). Motivated by the real-world prevalence of open-weight MoE backbones being repackaged into proprietary products (cites McKinsey/IBM figures that 63% of organizations use open-source AI models, 60% source AI tools from open-source ecosystems, and OpenRouter aggregating 300+ models).
limitations: The paper's own scoping/limitations, stated or directly evidenced in-text: (1) the threat model requires white-box (weights/activations/router-logit) access to a *related* open-weight surrogate model in the offline stage -- the attack is not zero-knowledge end-to-end, it depends on transfer from a same-family surrogate; (2) attack strength is architecture-dependent -- coarse-grained models with fewer experts per layer (e.g., Phi-3.5-MoE, 16 experts) show markedly lower ASR (34.7%) because safety and general-linguistic capability are more entangled when there are fewer experts to disentangle; (3) universal (cross-prompt) suffixes underperform per-prompt individually-optimized suffixes (69.3% vs. 80.7% ASR upper bound), showing the universal setting sacrifices some effectiveness for generality; (4) the target-expert proportion is capped at 20% by the authors' own choice because larger proportions risk misidentifying/suppressing polysemantic syntactic experts and increase gradient-tensor computational overhead, i.e., there is an explicit efficacy/coherence/cost trade-off rather than a free win; (5) evaluation ASR relies on an automated judge pipeline (Llama-Guard-3-8B + Qwen3Guard-Gen-8B with human adjudication only for 'ambiguous cases'), which is standard practice but still judge-dependent; (6) defenses are only sketched at a conceptual level (routing-aware regularization during training, inference-time router-logit anomaly detection) with no defense actually implemented or evaluated in the paper.

datasets_used: LLM-LAT dataset (4,950 paired harmful-completion/safe-refusal pairs; 600 sampled for expert profiling)
Alpaca and WikiText-2 (mixed, 600 samples, benign corpus for utility-aware filtering)
StrongREJECT benchmark (313 malicious prompts, used for ASR evaluation)
CoLA, RTE, WinoGrande, OpenBookQA, ARC-Challenge (5 NLU benchmarks for general utility evaluation)
quantitative_results: Average ASR: RouteHijack 69.3% vs. Baseline 7.1%, GCG 21.9%, SAFEx 33.3%, SteerMoE 17.0%
Peak per-model ASR: 89.1% on DeepSeek-MoE-16B-Chat (RouteHijack); 87.2% on Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B-Chat
Average utility drop across 5 NLU benchmarks: 1.3% (with utility-aware filtering) vs. 10.8% (without the filter, ablation)
Boundary-token routing shift: average TESR -28.75%, average THPR +28.51%
Global (across-generation) routing shift: safety-expert frequency -23.44% avg, harmful-expert frequency +22.87% avg
Ternary-loss ablation average ASR: full pipeline 69.3%; w/o Lpromote 50.2%; w/o Lsuppress 7.1% (6/7 models >50% incoherent output); w/o Lrefusal 29.1%
Profiling ablation: response-driven 69.3% avg ASR vs. prompt-driven 30.5% avg ASR
Universal vs. individual suffix: 69.3% (universal) vs. 80.7% (per-prompt upper bound, 226/280 on 40 sampled prompts x 7 models)
Sibling-model zero-shot transfer: average ASR 27.7% (baseline) -> 61.2% (RouteHijack); e.g. Qwen1.5-Chat->Wikihow variant 18.5%->83.3%; Mixtral->notux-8x7b-v1 23.9%->41.7%
MoE-VLM zero-shot transfer: average ASR 2.47% (baseline) -> 38.7% (RouteHijack); e.g. Qwen3.5-35B-A3B 0%->35.3%, Kimi-VL-A3B-Instruct 7.4%->49.2%
baselines_compared: GCG (Greedy Coordinate Gradient, input-only optimization-based jailbreak, 500-step budget targeting an affirmative prefix)
SAFEx (white-box, prunes safety-critical experts profiled over 20,000 jailbreak prompts)
SteerMoE (white-box, perturbs expert logits pre-Top-K routing at inference)
